# Intelligent and automatic irrigation system based on internet of things using fuzzy control technology

[*Scientific Reports*](/srep)
**volume 15**, Article number: 14577 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

5545 Accesses

1 Citations

1 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Agriculture is a vital sector in the global economy but also a major contributor to water consumption and wastage due to inefficient irrigation techniques. To address this issue, smart irrigation systems using the Internet of Things (IoT) have emerged as a solution for optimizing water use. These systems utilize real-time sensor data to improve irrigation efficiency and agricultural productivity. This paper presents an automatic, low-cost intelligent irrigation system based on a fuzzy rule-based inference approach and an energy-aware routing algorithm. The proposed system determines the optimal irrigation method using sensor data and ensures efficient information transmission through a fast fuzzy-based routing mechanism. Additionally, it enables remote monitoring and control via mobile devices, enhancing user convenience. The system is designed to be intelligent, cost-effective, and portable, making it suitable for various agricultural applications such as greenhouses and farms. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing algorithms, including DLQR, SPIS, and FWIS, in terms of network lifetime and power consumption. By improving water efficiency and reducing resource wastage, this research contributes to sustainable agriculture. The proposed system provides a scalable and adaptable solution for modern farming, ensuring better irrigation management while conserving essential resources.

## Introduction

Agriculture is a vital sector in the global economy, supporting the livelihoods of billions of people and providing the food necessary to sustain the world’s population. However, the agriculture sector also faces critical challenges related to water scarcity[1](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR1 "Pandiyaraju, V., Logambigai, R., Ganapathy, S. & Kannan, A. An energy efficient routing algorithm for WSNs using intelligent fuzzy rules in precision agriculture. Wireless Pers. Commun. 112 (1), 243–259 (2020)."),[2](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR2 "Rezaeipanah, A., Amiri, P., Nazari, H., Mojarad, M. & Parvin, H. An energy-aware hybrid approach for wireless sensor networks using re-clustering-based multi-hop routing. Wireless Pers. Commun. 120 (4), 3293–3314 (2021)."). The global water crisis, driven by over-extraction, climate change, and inefficient usage, has made water conservation a pressing priority. According to the United Nations, by 2025, two-thirds of the world’s population could face water stress[3](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR3 "Jiménez, A. F., Cárdenas, P. F. & Jiménez, F. Intelligent IoT-multiagent precision irrigation approach for improving water use efficiency in irrigation systems at farm and district scales. Comput. Electron. Agric. 192, 106635 (2022)."). This is particularly alarming given that agriculture accounts for approximately 70% of global freshwater consumption. The excessive use of water in irrigation, coupled with traditional farming practices that often lack efficiency and adaptability, exacerbates the problem, leading to water wastage and environmental degradation[4](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR4 "Wu, X. et al. Dual-channel event-triggered prescribed performance adaptive fuzzy time-varying formation tracking control for nonlinear multi-agent systems. Fuzzy Sets Syst. 498, 109140 (2025)."),[5](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR5 "Khummanee, S., Wiangsamut, S., Sorntepa, P. & Jaiboon, C. Automated smart farming for orchids with the internet of things and fuzzy logic. In 2018 International Conference on Information Technology (InCIT) (pp. 1–6). IEEE. (2018), October.").

Traditional irrigation systems, which rely on time-based or manual methods, have several disadvantages. These methods often do not take into account dynamic environmental factors such as soil moisture levels, rainfall patterns, and temperature variations, leading to inefficient water usage[6](#ref-CR6 "Pandiyaraju, V., Ganapathy, S., Mohith, N. & Kannan, A. An optimal energy utilization model for precision agriculture in WSNs using multi-objective clustering and deep learning. J. King Saud University-Computer Inform. Sci. 35 (10), 101803 (2023)."),[7](#ref-CR7 "Sharma, R. P., Dharavath, R. & Edla, D. R. IoFT-FIS: internet of farm things based prediction for crop pest infestation using optimized fuzzy inference system. Internet Things. 21, 100658 (2023)."),[8](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR8 "Cai, J., Guo, D. & Wang, W. Adaptive fault-tolerant control of uncertain systems with unknown actuator failures and input delay. Meas. Control, 00202940241289217. (2024)."). Furthermore, many farmers still use outdated technologies that are not capable of adjusting irrigation schedules in response to changing weather or soil conditions. As a result, crop yields may suffer from either under-irrigation or over-irrigation, which in turn affects agricultural productivity and resource sustainability[2](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR2 "Rezaeipanah, A., Amiri, P., Nazari, H., Mojarad, M. & Parvin, H. An energy-aware hybrid approach for wireless sensor networks using re-clustering-based multi-hop routing. Wireless Pers. Commun. 120 (4), 3293–3314 (2021).").

Climate-related smart agriculture, which combines IoT, sensor technologies, and data analytics, has emerged as a promising solution to optimize irrigation and improve crop management. These systems rely on climate data, soil moisture measurements, and weather forecasts to adjust irrigation schedules dynamically, reducing water wastage and enhancing productivity[1](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR1 "Pandiyaraju, V., Logambigai, R., Ganapathy, S. & Kannan, A. An energy efficient routing algorithm for WSNs using intelligent fuzzy rules in precision agriculture. Wireless Pers. Commun. 112 (1), 243–259 (2020)."),[10](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR10 "Adamu, M., Mayowa, A. & Jonah, I. Decision support system and fuzzy logic controller for capillary irrigation system. Am. J. Comput. Eng. 6 (1), 14–28 (2023)."). By integrating smart farming techniques with data-driven decision-making, climate-smart agriculture aims to mitigate the negative impacts of climate change on crop yields, making agriculture more sustainable and resilient[11](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR11 "Zhong, Y., Chen, L., Dan, C. & Rezaeipanah, A. A systematic survey of data mining and big data analysis in internet of things. J. Supercomputing. 78 (17), 18405–18453 (2022)."). To address these issues, various irrigation methods have been developed over the years. Conventional systems include surface irrigation, sprinkler systems, and drip irrigation, each with their own limitations. While drip irrigation is more water-efficient than traditional methods, it still relies heavily on manual monitoring and adjustments[12](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR12 "Neugebauer, M., Akdeniz, C., Demir, V. & Yurdem, H. Fuzzy logic control for watering system. Sci. Rep. 13 (1), 18485 (2023)."),[13](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR13 "Lu, S., & Xiao, X. Neuromorphic computing for smart agriculture. Agriculture. 14(11), 1977 (2024)."). More recent advancements in irrigation technologies have focused on integrating sensors and automation to improve water management. The Internet of Things (IoT) has emerged as a transformative technology for addressing these challenges by enabling the automation and remote monitoring of agricultural systems[1](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR1 "Pandiyaraju, V., Logambigai, R., Ganapathy, S. & Kannan, A. An energy efficient routing algorithm for WSNs using intelligent fuzzy rules in precision agriculture. Wireless Pers. Commun. 112 (1), 243–259 (2020)."),[3](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR3 "Jiménez, A. F., Cárdenas, P. F. & Jiménez, F. Intelligent IoT-multiagent precision irrigation approach for improving water use efficiency in irrigation systems at farm and district scales. Comput. Electron. Agric. 192, 106635 (2022)."). IoT-based smart irrigation systems can collect real-time data from a variety of sensors, such as soil moisture and temperature, and use this data to optimize irrigation schedules[14](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR14 "Gong, J. & Rezaeipanah, A. A fuzzy delay-bandwidth guaranteed routing algorithm for video conferencing services over SDN networks. Multimedia Tools Appl. 82 (17), 25585–25614 (2023)."),[15](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR15 "Zhang, H. & Li, M. Towards an intelligent and automatic irrigation system based on internet of things with authentication feature in VANET. J. Inform. Secur. Appl. 88, 103927 (2025)."). Despite the promising potential of IoT, existing systems still face limitations in energy efficiency, reliability, and adaptability to different agricultural environments[15](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR15 "Zhang, H. & Li, M. Towards an intelligent and automatic irrigation system based on internet of things with authentication feature in VANET. J. Inform. Secur. Appl. 88, 103927 (2025)."),[16](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR16 "Wang, T., Wang, H., Wang, C. & Hu, H. A novel PID controller for BLDCM speed control using dual fuzzy logic systems with HSA optimization. Sci. Rep. 12 (1), 11316 (2022).").

This paper proposes an intelligent and automatic irrigation system that leverages IoT and fuzzy control technology[9](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR9 "Han, A., Yang, Q., Chen, Y. & Li, J. Failure-distribution-dependent h ∞ fuzzy fault-tolerant control for nonlinear multilateral teleoperation system with communication delays. Electronics 13 (17), 3454 (2024).") to optimize water consumption. The system integrates a fuzzy rule-based inference engine to process sensor data and make irrigation decisions. Additionally, a fuzzy-based system is trained to determine the most appropriate irrigation method based on the environmental conditions. In addition, a fast fuzzy-based routing algorithm is designed in the existing system for information transmission. The system is designed to be low-cost, energy-efficient, and portable, allowing farmers to monitor and control irrigation remotely using mobile communication technologies like Global System for Mobile (GSM).

The proposed intelligent irrigation system introduces a novel integration of fuzzy logic-based irrigation control, deep neural networks (DNNs) for decision-making, and an energy-efficient Open Shortest Path First (OSPF)-based routing mechanism to optimize water usage and enhance network lifetime. Unlike conventional systems that rely solely on predefined threshold-based decision-making, our approach leverages historical and real-time data to dynamically determine the best irrigation schedule. The Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) ensures adaptive and precise irrigation control, while the DNN model refines predictions based on environmental conditions. Additionally, the enhanced OSPF-based routing algorithm improves network energy efficiency by selecting optimal communication paths. These innovations collectively enhance the scalability, adaptability, and efficiency of IoT-driven irrigation systems, setting our method apart from existing solutions.

The main contributions of this work are as follows:

The development of an intelligent irrigation system based on fuzzy control, enabling real-time decision-making for optimized water use.

Integration of DNNs with a fuzzy-based system to enhance adaptability and accuracy in selecting the optimal irrigation method based on dynamic environmental conditions.

A fast fuzzy-based routing algorithm designed to improve energy efficiency and extend the network lifetime in WSNs.

A low-cost, energy-efficient, and portable system suitable for various agricultural applications, including farms and greenhouses.

The use of IoT-driven smart farming techniques to enable remote monitoring and management, improving irrigation precision and resource utilization.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 reviews related work in the field of IoT-based irrigation systems. Section 3 summarizes some concepts and definitions relevant to this study. Section 4 describes the system architecture and design. Section 5 presents the simulation setup and results, while Sect. 6 concludes the paper and discusses future work.

## Literature reviews

Existing IoT-based intelligent irrigation systems using fuzzy control optimize irrigation through real-time data and decision-making, leveraging sensors and fuzzy logic to manage environmental uncertainties[17](#ref-CR17 "Wang, M., Kang, J., Liu, W., Li, M., Su, J., Fang, Z., Li, X., Shang, L., Zhang, F. & Guo, C. Design and study of mine silo drainage method based on fuzzy control and Avoiding Peak Filling Valley strategy. Sci. Rep. 14(1), 9300 (2024).  "),[18](#ref-CR18 "Yue, S., Xu, N., Zhang, L. & Zhao, N. Observer-based event-triggered adaptive fuzzy hierarchical sliding mode fault-tolerant control for uncertain under-actuated nonlinear systems. Int. J. Fuzzy Syst. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s40815-024-01834-9
                  
                 (2024)."),[19](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR19 "Pandiyaraju, V., Perumal, P. S., Kannan, A. & Ramesh, L. S. Smart terrace gardening with intelligent roof control algorithm for water conservation. Pakistan J. Agricultural Sci. 54 (2), 451–455 (2017)."). However, they face challenges like scalability, high costs, energy inefficiency, and limited adaptability to diverse conditions. Integrating advanced machine learning with fuzzy systems remains a gap, requiring research to enhance efficiency and precision[20](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR20 "Zhu, B. et al. Observer-based reinforcement learning for optimal fault-tolerant consensus control of nonlinear multi-agent systems via a dynamic event-triggered mechanism. Inf. Sci. 689, 121350 (2025)."),[21](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR21 "Liu, C., Wang, J., Zhou, L. & Rezaeipanah, A. Solving the multi-objective problem of IoT service placement in fog computing using cuckoo search algorithm. Neural Process. Lett. 54 (3), 1823–1854 (2022)."). In the following, we provide an overview of the latest methods in this field[15](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR15 "Zhang, H. & Li, M. Towards an intelligent and automatic irrigation system based on internet of things with authentication feature in VANET. J. Inform. Secur. Appl. 88, 103927 (2025)."),[22](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR22 "Luo, J., Zhao, C., Chen, Q., & Li, G. Using deep belief network to construct the agricultural information system based on Internet of Things. J. Supercomput. 78(1), 379-405 (2022)."). Table [1](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#Tab1) presents a comparison of the existing methods of intelligent and automatic irrigation system based on the IoT using fuzzy control technology.

Chen et al.[23](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR23 "Chen, C. M., Zhang, Z., Ming-Tai Wu, J. & Lakshmanna, K. High utility periodic frequent pattern mining in multiple sequences. CMES-Computer Model. Eng. Sci. 137 (1), 733–759 (2023).") introduced MHUPFPS, an efficient algorithm for identifying high-utility patterns in period sequences. The study addresses existing challenges by defining three novel measures: utility, high support, and high-utility period sequence ratios. Additionally, a new upper bound, upSeqRa, and two innovative pruning properties were proposed. The algorithm leverages the HUPFPS-list structure to significantly reduce the search space, thereby enhancing its overall performance. Zhou et al.[24](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR24 "Zhou, J. et al. A lightweight energy consumption ensemble-based botnet detection model for IoT/6G networks. Sustain. Energy Technol. Assess. 60, 103454 (2023).") presented an ensemble-based detection framework designed for identifying botnets in IoT networks. By leveraging machine learning techniques, the model ensures effective protection of network infrastructure. It employs univariate feature selection to identify critical features and integrates them into an ensemble framework for robust detection. Given the substantial energy consumption caused by botnet attacks in IoT devices, the proposed model not only detects and mitigates these threats but also promotes energy efficiency, contributing to the sustainability of IoT networks.

Bukhari et al.[25](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR25 "Bukhari, M., Athar, S. O., Ullah, M. & Aman, M. N. A fuzzy Logic-based smart irrigation controller for precision agriculture. IEEE Internet Things J. 11 (22), 37257–37268 (2024).") introduced a precision irrigation controller utilizing a FIS to optimize irrigation. The FIS employed soil moisture deviation and crop coefficients as inputs, leveraging a 28-rule base to calculate irrigation volume increments. While Discrete Linear Quadratic Regulator (DLQR) provided high accuracy, it was computationally intensive for IoT applications. In contrast, Proportional-Integral (PI) controllers were lightweight but less accurate, highlighting the FIS’s balanced performance. Rahayu[26](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR26 "Rahayu, A. U. Smart irrigation system with fuzzy logic on sunflower plants based on internet of things. J. Energy Electr. Eng. 6 (1), 74–81 (2024).") developed an IoT-based smart irrigation system using fuzzy logic for sunflower cultivation. The system integrated sensors, fuzzy logic (via the mamdani method), and IoT technology to automate water monitoring and regulation. This approach aimed to enhance productivity and minimize resource wastage. The research employed an experimental model combining hardware and software to create an efficient and sustainable irrigation solution.

Zheng et al.[27](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR27 "Zheng, Y., Jiang, Z., Kozlov, O. V. & Kondratenko, Y. P. Fuzzy automatic control of the irrigation process for the IoT-based smart farming systems. J. Ambient Intell. Smart Environ., (Preprint), 1–27. (2024).") developed an advanced IoT-based fuzzy control system for irrigation in smart farming complexes. The system employed a hierarchical two-level IoT framework and two-channel fuzzy logic control to ensure precise soil moisture and pH regulation. This approach enhanced microbial activity, nutrient absorption, and water efficiency, leading to improved crop yields and sustainable resource use. The system demonstrated high performance, adaptability, and customization for diverse agricultural applications. Benzaouia et al.[28](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR28 "Benzaouia, M., Hajji, B., Mellit, A. & Rabhi, A. Fuzzy-IoT smart irrigation system for precision scheduling and monitoring. Comput. Electron. Agric. 215, 108407 (2023).") developed a Smart Precision Irrigation System (SPIS) integrating a Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC) and LoRa-based data transmission for real-time monitoring and control. The system processed data from sensors—soil moisture, temperature, solar irradiance, and rainfall—through Mamdani fuzzy inference with trapezoidal and triangular membership functions. Combining fuzzy logic and IoT enhanced irrigation decisions, optimized scheduling, and addressed limitations of conventional systems, ensuring improved performance and communication with farmers.

Hoque et al.[29](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR29 "Hoque, M. J., Islam, M. S. & Khaliluzzaman, M. A Fuzzy logic-and Internet of things-based smart irrigation system. Eng. Proc. 58 (1), 93, (2023).") proposed an intelligent irrigation system integrating fuzzy logic and IoT to automate water pump control. The system used climate sensors connected to an Arduino and a FIS to monitor soil moisture and climate conditions. The FIS analyzed sensor data to adjust pump speed, ensuring precise irrigation. This autonomous approach reduced water and electricity use, eliminated human intervention, and enhanced crop productivity by maintaining optimal soil moisture levels. Dhumale et al.[30](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR30 "Dhumale, R. B. et al. Fuzzy internet of things-based water irrigation system. Agricultural Eng. International: CIGR J., 25(2). (2023).") proposed an IoT-based smart Fuzzy Water Irrigation System (FWIS) with Master and Sensor Nodes. The system operated in Manual Mode (MM) via a web server for remote control and Automatic Mode (AM) using fuzzy logic. In AM, fuzzy logic determined pump activation times based on soil temperature and moisture data. This approach optimized water distribution, combining wireless communication and intelligent control for efficient irrigation management.

Xie et al.[31](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR31 "Xie, J. et al. Smart fuzzy irrigation system for litchi orchards. Comput. Electron. Agric. 201, 107287 (2022).") proposed a smart irrigation fuzzy control system incorporating an improved Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm to enhance orchard crop production. The system addressed challenges like local optima in standard PSO and the complexity of modeling nonlinear irrigation systems. By optimizing soil moisture levels, the approach prevented both hypoxia and yield loss, improving irrigation efficiency and sustainability in orchard management. Jdey[32](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR32 "Jdey, I. Trusted smart irrigation system based on fuzzy IoT and blockchain. In International Conference on Service-Oriented Computing (pp. 154–165). Cham: Springer Nature Switzerland. (2022).") introduced a fuzzy computational analysis for IoT-based smart irrigation systems, addressing security and data privacy challenges. The system employed distributed sensors to collect temperature and humidity data, processed via the Mamdani fuzzy logic model for valve control decisions. Blockchain technology ensured secure data transfer and trusted device access. By integrating fuzzy logic for decision-making and Blockchain for security, the approach enhanced efficiency and reliability in irrigation management.

Mhaned et al.[33](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR33 "Mhaned, A., Mouatassim, S., El Haji, M. & Benhra, J. Low-cost smart irrigation system based on internet of things and fuzzy logic. In International Conference on Smart Applications and Data Analysis (78–89). Cham: Springer International Publishing. (2022).") developed a low-cost irrigation system to minimize water usage and human intervention. The system collected soil moisture, temperature, humidity, and rainfall data, transmitted via the Message Queue Telemetry Transport Protocol (MQTT) protocol to a Raspberry Pi and ThingSpeak for user monitoring. Users could automate irrigation based on parameters or manually control valves via a mobile app. Fuzzy logic ensured optimal water distribution by defining rules for precise irrigation scheduling. Benyezza et al.[34](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR34 "Benyezza, H., Bouhedda, M. & Rebouh, S. Zoning irrigation smart system based on fuzzy control technology and IoT for water and energy saving. J. Clean. Prod. 302, 127001 (2021).") proposed an IoT-based zoning irrigation system aimed at optimizing plant growth and minimizing water and energy use. The system utilized a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) to collect soil humidity and temperature data from various greenhouse zones, transmitted via radio frequency communication to a Raspberry Pi server. A FLC analyzed the data to make intelligent irrigation decisions, ensuring efficient resource utilization and improved growing conditions.

Mohammed et al.[35](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR35 "Mohammed, B., Bekkay, H., Migan-Dubois, A., Adel, M. & Rabhi, A. An intelligent irrigation system based on fuzzy logic control: A case study for Moroccan oriental climate region. In 2nd international conference on Embedded Systems and Artificial Intelligence (ESAI’21). (2021).") introduced an intelligent irrigation system tailored to the climatic conditions of Oriental Morocco. The system employed fuzzy logic to determine watering durations adaptively and precisely based on inputs like air temperature, solar radiation, and soil moisture. Data were collected using analog and digital sensors, enabling the system to optimize irrigation practices while considering environmental conditions for efficient agricultural water management. Suguna[36](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR36 "Suguna, M. Fuzzy logic controller based IoT for smart irrigation system. In 2021 International Conference on Advancements in Electrical, Electronics, Communication, Computing and Automation (ICAECA) (pp. 1–5). IEEE. (2021).") proposed an IoT-based irrigation system designed to reduce watering frequency while boosting productivity. The system utilized an adaptive fuzzy regulator to analyze environmental data, such as soil moisture and climate conditions, collected through specialized sensors. By applying fuzzy logic rules, it controlled water flow, optimizing irrigation timing and frequency. This approach enhanced water efficiency and supported improved crop production.

Krishnan et al.[37](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR37 "Krishnan, R. S. et al. Fuzzy logic based smart irrigation system using internet of things. J. Clean. Prod. 252, 119902 (2020).") introduced a GSM-enabled smart irrigation system for farmers, providing status updates on soil humidity, environmental temperature, and motor power source. A FLC processed inputs like soil moisture, temperature, and humidity to manage motor operation. The system conserved power by switching off the motor during rainfall and protected crops using rain panels. This approach enhanced irrigation efficiency and supported sustainable farming practices. Pezol et al.[38](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR38 "Pezol, N. S., Adnan, R. & Tajjudin, M. Design of an internet of things (iot) based smart irrigation and fertilization system using fuzzy logic for chili plant. In 2020 IEEE International Conference on Automatic Control and Intelligent Systems (I2CACIS) (pp. 69–73). IEEE. (2020).") developed a smart irrigation and fertilization system for chili plants using IoT and fuzzy logic. The system utilized Wi-Fi for connectivity and a FLC to regulate the flow rates of water, alkali, and acid solutions, maintaining optimal soil moisture and pH levels. Inputs included soil moisture and pH, with outputs controlling alkali, neutral, and acid flow rates. This approach ensured precise resource management for improved crop health.

Farooq et al.[39](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR39 "Farooq, M., Hussain, A., Hashim, S., Yang, L. & Ali, M. Automated Irrigation system based on irrigation gates using fuzzy logic. In 2020 International Conference on Internet of Things and Intelligent Applications (ITIA) (pp. 1–5). IEEE. (2020).") proposed a precision irrigation system using a smart gate equipped with water level and flow sensors. WSN nodes collected soil moisture, temperature, and humidity data, transmitted to a Firebase Realtime Database. fuzzy logic evaluated this data to calculate water requirements based on a fuzzy rule-base, ensuring optimal irrigation. A mobile dashboard provided farmers with real-time data and control, enhancing irrigation efficiency and resource management. Pandiyaraju et al.[6](/articles/s41598-025-98137-2#ref-CR6 "Pandiyaraju, V., Ganapathy, S., Mohith, N. & Kannan, A. An optimal energy utilization model for precision agriculture in WSNs using multi-objective clustering and deep learning. J. King Saud University-Computer Inform. Sci. 35 (10), 101803 (2023).") proposed a multi-objective clustering approach for grouping sensor nodes in WSNs. They introduced the Election-based Aquila Optimizer (EAO), combining the Aquila Optimizer (AO) and Election-Based Optimization Algorithm (EBOA), to optimize Cluster Head (CH) selection. Additionally, they integrated EAO with a convolutional neural network, forming an Optimized Convolutional Neural Network (O-CNN),

# Innovations in Drip Irrigation: Latest Trends and Technologies

Feb 2nd 2024

As the agriculture industry faces climate change, water scarcity, urbanization, and the need for resource conservation practices, the drip irrigation system stands out as an invaluable solution. [Drip irrigation](https://www.dripworks.com/drip-irrigation), an efficient method of delivering water directly to plant roots, has recently undergone significant innovations. This irrigation system offers numerous benefits to farmers, landscapers, and their plants by providing precise water delivery. It is revolutionizing the agriculture industry, conserving resources, and promoting sustainable practices.

In this blog, we explore the importance of the drip irrigation system and the latest advancements in this system that make it a sustainable and efficient watering method worldwide.

## Importance of Drip Irrigation in Agriculture

Drip irrigation, also called micro-irrigation, is an innovative and economical irrigation system that delivers water directly to plant root zones in a controlled amount. Unlike traditional watering methods such as sprinkler irrigation, a drip irrigation system reduces water wastage due to run-off and evaporation. The [importance of drip irrigation](https://www.dripworks.com/blog/advantages-of-drip-irrigation) in farming cannot be overstated. This method uses 50% less water than other traditional irrigation methods. It ensures effective water distribution by supplying water directly to roots, which minimizes evaporation.

In addition to water conservation, drip irrigation saves nutrients as it offers precise control over fertilizer and nutrient delivery. Drip irrigation systems can be automated, which reduces the need for labor and provides farmers more flexibility. With so much efficiency, it is no surprise that drip irrigation has become popular among farmers and homeowners worldwide as it contributes to sustainable agriculture.

## Latest Trends and Technologies in Drip Irrigation

Let's discuss the innovations in the drip irrigation industry that are taking its efficiency to the next level.

### **Precision Farming with Drip Irrigation**

Technological advancements have enabled precise control over drip irrigation. Farmers can customize specific parameters such as irrigation timing, duration, and water flow rate depending on crop requirements and weather conditions. They can adapt the system to the watering and nutritional needs of plants. This precise approach ensures the water is slowly delivered to the plants when needed, reducing unnecessary water usage, and increasing water efficiency and yield. So, drip irrigation systems play a great role in precision farming.

### **Sensor-based Systems**

Sensors play an essential role in the evolution of smart drip irrigation systems. Sensor-based drip systems use sensing technology to monitor and maximize water usage. Various types of sensors are used to monitor soil moisture, plant health, weather, and other factors that affect plants' watering needs. For example, soil sensors provide data about soil moisture content, which helps farmers adjust their irrigation schedule accordingly. These sensors can be wired or wireless and can connect to a central controller or cloud-based system.

### **Automation**

Automation using [timers](https://www.dripworks.com/drip-irrigation/timers) and controllers in drip irrigation saves you time and water. You can install a [battery-operated](https://www.dripworks.com/drip-irrigation/timers/battery), [electronic](https://www.dripworks.com/drip-irrigation/timers/electronic-ac), or [solar timer](https://www.dripworks.com/drip-irrigation/timers/solar) in your system and provide instructions to irrigate crops at a particular time and rate. With this innovation, you can run errands and even take a vacation without worrying about your plants. This will give your plants the right amount of water when needed, reduce labor costs, and maximize water efficiency.

### **Smart Drip Irrigation System**

Smart drip irrigation systems leverage AI and IoT solutions to optimize water delivery and irrigation schedules. Smart systems analyze crop needs, soil moisture levels, and weather patterns and provide real-time field data to adjust water delivery and adapt your system to changing conditions. This integration reduces water wastage and enhances crop health. For example, Netafim offers a cloud-based platform that integrates AI into drip irrigation systems and sensors to improve water delivery and make informed decisions.

For more information about AI and IoT solutions, read our guide on the [Role of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Smart Irrigation Systems](https://www.dripworks.com/blogthe-role-of-artificial-intelligence-ai-in-smart-irrigation-systems-/)!

### **Solar-Powered Drip Irrigation System**

With the rise of drip irrigation installation in gardens, landscapes, and farms, solar-powered drip irrigation systems are also becoming popular among growers. In solar-powered drip systems, power generated by solar panels is used to run water pumps and controllers. These systems provide an eco-friendly alternative to conventional energy resources.

Solar-powered drip irrigation is a cost-effective system. It reduces operational costs and requires minimal maintenance. This system can be installed on a broad range of scales, from vegetable gardens to large farms.

### **Subsurface Drip Irrigation**

[Subsurface Drip Irrigation](https://extension.colostate.edu/topic-areas/agriculture/subsurface-drip-irrigation-sdi-4-716/#:~:text=Subsurface%20drip%20irrigation%20is%20a,incidence%20of%20weeds%20and%20disease.) (SDI) is a highly efficient and low-pressure system that uses buried drip tubes to deliver water to plants. This is an underground irrigation system that offers multiple advantages. SDI reduces evaporation, keeps the soil surface dry, suppresses weed growth, improves fertilizer uptake, and offers control over water application.

### **Drip Fertigation**

Fertigation is another emerging trend in the drip irrigation industry. Fertilizing through drip irrigation is called fertigation. It is a technique of supplying soluble fertilizers and micronutrients straight to the plant's roots through the drip system. This fertilizing technique is a sustainable farming practice that allows farmers to control nutrient dosage according to plant needs, soil structure, and season. Drip fertigation improves nutrient efficiency and reduces environmental impact.

## The Future of Drip Irrigation System

Drip irrigation is an innovative method proven more effective than traditional methods because it applies water directly to root zones. The continuous innovations in drip irrigation systems are revolutionizing agriculture and promoting sustainable and precision farming practices. A well-designed drip irrigation system reduces water waste, conserves energy, minimizes environmental impact, saves time, and improves crop yield. We can anticipate more advancements in the future that maximize water usage, conserve resources, and enhance agriculture efficiency.

Advanced technologies such as sensors, automation, and remote monitoring systems are transforming the drip irrigation industry. According to a report published by [MarketsandMarkets](https://www.marketsandmarkets.com/Market-Reports/drip-irrigation-systems-market-217216582.html), the global drip irrigation market size is anticipated to grow rapidly, rising from $5.2 Billion in 2022 to $8.3 Billion in 2023, growing at a CAGR of 9.8%. Future technological advancements may include more smart systems available in the market that integrate with IoT solutions and satellite imagery for greater precision.

## Conclusion

Drip irrigation emerges as an effective solution to increasing challenges in agriculture. All the latest innovations are helping to make drip irrigation more efficient, affordable, and profitable. Embracing innovative irrigation systems has become paramount for environmentally friendly agriculture. By adopting these innovations, landscapers and farmers can reduce water wastage, increase yield, and contribute to a sustainable future.

[DripWorks](https://www.dripworks.com/) offers the best drip irrigation systems for gardens, landscapes, and farms. Whether you're looking for a complete [drip irrigation kit](https://www.dripworks.com/drip-irrigation/irrigation-kits) or individual smart components, you will find all drip irrigation solutions tailored to your budget and crop needs.

You can [contact](https://www.dripworks.com/contact-us) our irrigation experts for any product queries and guidance!

# The Latest Trends in Smart Irrigation Technology

![A farmer holding their hand underneath an irrigation system. They check the system, using a tablet in their other hand.](https://www.farmhq.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/CODAFarmTechnologies-335060-Farmer-Water-Irrigation-Blogbanner1-jpg.webp)

![A farmer holding their hand underneath an irrigation system. They check the system, using a tablet in their other hand.](https://www.farmhq.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/CODAFarmTechnologies-335060-Farmer-Water-Irrigation-Blogbanner1-jpg.webp)

Smart irrigation technology has revolutionized the way we manage water resources and maintain healthy vegetation. By leveraging innovative sensors, controllers, and data analytics, smart irrigation systems optimize water usage, reduce waste, and enhance plant growth while minimizing environmental impact. Explore the latest trends in smart irrigation technology to see what the future of sustainable water management and irrigation practices holds.

## Internet of Things (IoT) Integration

Integrating Internet of Things (IoT) capabilities has produced IoT-enabled sensors and controllers that collect real-time data on soil moisture levels, weather conditions, plant health, and water flow rates. This data allows farmers to precisely adjust their automated irrigation systems. IoT integration also enables remote monitoring, predictive analytics, and intelligent decision-making for efficient water management by connecting various irrigation system components to a central network.

## Weather-Based Irrigation Scheduling

Weather-based irrigation scheduling is a trend that focuses on optimizing irrigation schedules based on local weather forecasts and real-time climate data. For example, users can adjust watering times, durations, and frequencies to align with specific weather conditions by incorporating weather sensors and forecasting algorithms into [hose reel irrigation systems](https://www.farmhq.com/hose-reels/). This approach ensures that plants receive the right amount of water at the right time, reducing water waste, preventing overwatering, and promoting healthy plant growth.

## Soil Moisture Monitoring

Soil moisture monitoring technology has become a fundamental component of smart irrigation systems, enabling precise irrigation control based on soil moisture levels. Sensors embedded in the soil measure moisture content at various depths, providing insights into plant water requirements and root zone hydration. By maintaining optimal soil moisture levels, farmers can conserve water, prevent waterlogging, and promote efficient nutrient uptake by plants, leading to healthier and more resilient vegetation.

## Data Analytics and Automation

Data analytics and automation play a vital role in the evolution of smart irrigation technology, enabling predictive modeling, trend analysis, and autonomous decision-making processes. By harnessing big data and machine learning algorithms, smart irrigation systems can adapt irrigation strategies based on historical data, user preferences, and environmental factors. Automation features such as programmable schedules, adaptive watering algorithms, and self-adjusting valves streamline irrigation operations and ensure consistent water distribution across landscapes.

## Water Conservation and Sustainability

A focus on water conservation and sustainability sits at the core of smart irrigation technology. Smart irrigation systems contribute to environmental conservation efforts and support sustainable irrigation practices by optimizing water usage, minimizing runoff, and reducing water waste. The ability to tailor irrigation schedules to specific plant needs, soil conditions, and weather patterns promotes efficient water utilization and fosters a greener approach to landscape management.

These latest trends in smart irrigation technology are driving innovation in water management practices, enhancing irrigation efficiency, and promoting sustainable agriculture and landscaping practices. The evolution of smart irrigation technology holds the promise of transforming the way we approach water use in agriculture, urban landscaping, and environmental stewardship, paving the way for a more sustainable and water-conscious future.

## Breadcrumb

# Water-Efficient Technology Opportunity: Advanced Irrigation Controls

The [Federal Energy Management Program](/femp/federal-energy-management-program-legacy) (FEMP) identified advanced irrigation controls as a water-saving technology that is relevant to the federal sector, is commercially available, and offers significant water-savings potential.

This overview provides agencies with key information to deploy innovative products and systems that may otherwise be overlooked. It also helps agencies identify water-efficient technologies for consideration when entering into [energy savings performance contracts](/femp/energy-savings-performance-contracts-federal-agencies "Energy Savings Performance Contracts for Federal Agencies") or [utility energy service contracts](/femp/utility-program-and-utility-energy-service-contracts-federal-agencies "Utility Program and Utility Energy Service Contracts for Federal Agencies").

FEMP used the following considerations when selecting this technology.

## Technology Description

Advanced irrigation controllers manage irrigation with smart sensing technology that optimizes the irrigation schedule. Generally, two types of commercially available advanced irrigation controllers are used to replace standard timer-based controllers—weather-based controllers and soil moisture-based controllers.

Timer-based controllers operate on a fixed schedule and deliver a fixed amount of water. Advanced irrigation controllers optimize the irrigation schedule by applying water based on plant needs. Advanced irrigation controllers can have the ability to suspend irrigation if conditions such as adequate rainfall or soil moisture have been met. Figure 1 provides an overview of both technologies.

### Weather-Based Irrigation Controllers

Weather-based irrigation controllers (WBIC) use weather/climate data and landscape conditions (such as plant type, soil type, and slope) to determine the amount of irrigation to provide to the plants. To do this, the climate and landscape to determine the amount of irrigation to provide to the plants. To do this, the climate and landscape are used to calculate evapotranspiration (ET), which is the amount of water that evaporates from the soil surface and transpires from the plants.

ET is essentially the water demand that must be met for the plants to stay healthy. The actual amount of provided irrigation also depends on the weather conditions at the current moment. For example, short term weather conditions such as high winds and cloud cover may not be reflected in the ET depending on the calculation method, but these factors can affect the amount of watering done if a sensor is available.

Similarly, conditions such as rain may delay the next watering cycle. Weather data can be obtained from a nearby weather station (signal-based controller) or from locally measured data (onsite sensor-based controller).

Both types of WBICs can be stand-alone devices or devices that can be added to an existing timer-based system.

### Soil Moisture-Based Irrigation Controllers

Soil moisture-based irrigation controllers, also known as soil moisture sensors (SMSs), use direct measurements of soil moisture to adjust the irrigation schedule. If the soil is dry, the scheduled irrigation will occur as usual or the controller will provide additional watering if there is no scheduled irrigation. If the soil is adequately moist, the scheduled irrigation will be prevented or delayed.

Demand initiated controllers can modify the schedule while bypass controllers can only allow or prevent the scheduled events. These controllers have two important components: a sensor and an interface. The sensor is in contact with the soil and takes moisture measurements while the interface communicates the results to the controller.

These controllers can be stand-alone devices or devices that can be added to an existing timer-based system. Sometimes, SMSs are available as an add-on to WBICs.

![Infographic of various options for gathering irrigation data.](/sites/default/files/styles/full_article_width/public/2023-03/fig-1-irrigation-control.png?itok=IkDZRxBw)

Figure 1. Advanced Irrigation Control Options

Figure 1. Advanced Irrigation Control Options

## WaterSense Labeled Irrigation Control Technology

The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) WaterSense Program labels advanced WBIC and SMS products that meet performance requirements that ensure the controller meets the irrigation needs of plants while minimizing overwatering.

[WBICs with the WaterSense label](https://www.epa.gov/watersense/weather-based-irrigation-controllers) have been tested using the American National Standards Institute (ANSI)/American Society of Agricultural and Biological Engineers (ASABE) Standard S627: Weather-Based Landscape Irrigation Control Systems, which makes sure the product meets the minimum performance in the following areas:

##### **Irrigation Adequacy**

Evaluates the controller's ability to meet the necessary water requirements of the plants or landscape (based on the specific ET rate). WaterSense labeled controllers must score at least an 80% adequacy per irrigation zone to meet the performance threshold. Research indicates no significant difference between 80% and 100% irrigation regarding the appearance of turfgrass.

##### **Irrigation Excess**

Evaluates the controller's ability to avoid excess watering. WaterSense labeled controllers must score 10% or less irrigation excess per zone and 5% or less irrigation excess as an average across six irrigation zones to meet the performance threshold (if a controller has less than six zones, it must be programmed together with additional controllers to reach six zones). This approach allows variation in controller scheduling while still preventing overwatering.

[SMSs with the WaterSense label](https://www.epa.gov/watersense/soil-moisture-based-irrigation-controllers) have been tested using ANSI/ASABE Standard S633, Testing Protocol for Landscape Irrigation Soil Moisture-Based Control Technologies, which makes sure the product meets the minimum performance in the following areas:

##### **Function**

Determines whether the SMS can enable or disable irrigation at a variety of moisture levels. This makes sure the SMS will work properly regardless of landscape conditions.

##### **Precision**

Measures whether the SMS can consistently enable or disable an irrigation event at a pre-set moisture threshold.

##### **Soil Moisture Response**

Determines whether the SMS can adequately sense a change in soil moisture when moisture levels change.

##### **Freezing Functionality**

Evaluates whether the SMS properly functions after its sensor mechanism is frozen and thawed.

All WaterSense labeled WBICs and SMSs must have the following supplemental capabilities which were developed through feedback and discussions with WaterSense stakeholders and are in addition to the ANSI/ASABE S627 and S633 standards:

##### **System Memory**

The controller must be able to retain its programmed settings even when power is lost, and no backup battery is available. This type of data storage is known as non-volatile memory.

##### **Zone Control**

The controller must have zone-by-zone control for landscapes that have multiple areas with various watering requirements that need to be managed separately.

##### **Error Alerts**

The controller must be able to notify the user if it is not operating in smart mode (automatically adjusting irrigation based on sensor/signal input).

##### **Sensor Connections**

The controller must be able to connect to a rainfall device or SMSs (in the case of WBICs). These can be important components of an efficient irrigation system in many climate regions and multiple states have mandated their inclusion by law. EPA provides contacts for [irrigation professionals](https://lookforwatersense.epa.gov/pros/) with information about installation and mandates.

##### **Compliance With Utility Restrictions**

The controller must be able to accommodate watering restrictions. With the existence of utility-imposed watering restrictions, it is important that WBICs and SMSs are capable of watering efficiently while complying with these restrictions.

##### **Water Budget Mode**

The controller must include a percent adjust (aka "water budget") feature. This feature allows users to adjust the amount of water applied to the landscape without changing the detailed settings in the controller's program. A potential use of this feature is called "deficit watering," in which the landscape does not receive the full ET requirement and is essentially underwatered in favor of conserving water for other uses.

##### **Loss of Real-Time Data**

The controller must be able to revert to a conservative watering schedule if the product loses its real-time data input from the sensors. Historical weather data or the percent adjust (water budget) feature can be used.

##### **Automatic Return to Smart Mode**

The controller must be capable of automatically returning to smart mode if switched to manual mode. Often, controllers are turned to manual mode for troubleshooting or other reasons and inadvertently not returned to smart mode. This requirement makes sure the controller will automatically return to smart mode within a specified time period as designated by the manufacturer.

## Considerations for Selecting the Right Advanced Irrigation Controller

The following considerations are important when determining the appropriate advanced irrigation controller.

Understanding the size and boundaries of the landscape is a good first step toward selecting or designing an appropriate system. The size of the landscape factors into many of the considerations discussed below. Large landscapes could require multiple controllers with multiple zones, each with multiple sensors. Small landscapes could require different measurements or features than larger landscapes.

The location and accuracy of weather data is vital to the effectiveness of an WBIC. Commonly, Wi-Fi enabled controllers connect to local weather stations for data; thus, the quality of data from the weather station can impact the performance of the controller. Some manufacturers offer their own weather data service, which could require a subscription. If climate varies drastically over time or within a small area (such as in microclimates), an on-site sensor may be a more precise option for weather data. The possible sources of weather data for the specific application and the associated costs should be considered.

The connectivity requirements (wired, wireless, multiple sensors, etc.) of the irrigation system when selecting a SMSs should be considered. In the case of on-site measurements, the sensor must be connected to the controller. Commonly, this is done with a wired connection. For some applications, wireless communication can be used. In larger landscapes, multiple sensors may have to connect to the same controller.

The accuracy of the irrigation system needed to meet the landscape requirements should be considered. The accuracy and effectiveness of a controller can vary depending on the model. While products with the WaterSense label provide adequate irrigation without excess water use, performance standards are met if the controller functions within a given range. Actual performance of labeled controllers depends on a wide range of factors. Irrigation managers should expect to fine tune their system to the specific site conditions to optimize performance.

The desired control interface and desired functions should be considered when selecting an irrigation system. Many advanced controllers have software applications adapted for mobile phones. This software can allow for monitoring or control of an irrigation system from a remote location. Similarly, web or computer-based software is available for many controllers. Deficit watering, remote control of the irrigation program, and live monitoring are all possible features.

Some irrigation controllers store and communicate using cloud data, allowing for increased connectivity between controllers. This means that a network connection may be required. Some controllers have Wi-Fi modules available as an add-on. The advantages of cloud connection include the ability to communicate with other controllers in a large landscape and real-time data measurement and storage.

Carefully choosing the appropriate features for a controller can avoid unnecessary costs for unneeded features. The cost of a controller is highly dependent on the number of zones it can irrigate. Therefore, the number of zones required for the current landscape should be determined before selecting the appropriate controller. Selecting a controller with extra capacity could allow for adding future zones, but keep in mind this will increase the cost. Add-ons such as on-site soil moisture or weather sensors also can significantly affect the price. Software such as weather data services and maintenance add cost and should also be considered carefully.

Flow sensors/meters can monitor water flow rate and can be used to measure water use and detect unscheduled irrigation events or unusual water patterns such as leaks. Similarly, pressure sensors can monitor system pressures to detect line and sprinkler head leaks. For applications requiring quick and accurate fault detections, these supplemental devices could be helpful.

Building operators can potentially collect and interpret water data or control irrigation via the BAS platform. This allows for increased monitoring and control of the building's water resources.

Installation varies depending on the size of the system and the number of controllers/sensors/add-ons. Manufacturers provide installation instructions along with each model of controller, but [irrigation professionals](https://lookforwatersense.epa.gov/pros/) certified by a WaterSense labeled program can provide more information and assistance.

## Proven Savings Potential

There have been numerous studies performed by independent third parties to investigate the potential water savings associated with commercially available WBICs and SMSs controllers. Studies reviewed show a range of 15%–40% reduction in water use. The amount of water savings depends on many factors including climate, plant types, control programming, sensor types, and system size. These studies show that advanced irrigation controls demonstrate proven savings and are expected to provide even more benefits as the state-of-the-art technology advances. Specific findings of several research studies are briefly discussed in the following sections.

The American Society of Agricultural and Biological Engineers performed a [literature review](https://doi.org/10.13031/trans.13930) and found that the amount of savings from an advanced irrigation controller varies widely depending on climate, plant type, and the amount of human interaction with the technology (such as updating watering schedules and properly programming the controller). Controlled research studies found an average 50% water savings, but case studies on home and commercial landscapes found an average 30% water savings.

A [WaterSense case study](https://www.epa.gov/sites/default/files/2021-03/documents/ws-outdoor-water-smart-solution-large-landscapes.pdf) of a WBIC in the 372,000-square-foot Granite Park office complex in Dallas, Texas, found that 12.5 million gallons of water were saved in the first year (a 40% reduction). The water reduction resulted in a $47,000 savings within the year, with a simple payback period of 1.5 years.

Pacific Northwest National Laboratory [assessed the performance of a unique smart irrigation system](https://www.gsa.gov/cdnstatic/GPG_Findings_018-Weather-Station.pdf) that connects to a BAS in Battle Creek, Michigan. The unique BAS system was estimated at 66% water savings, showing the future potential of this currently advancing technology.

A [study from University of Florida](https://doi.org/10.13031/2013.41391 ) found that advanced irrigation controllers need to be set up and used properly to demonstrate the significant documented water savings. Both weather and soil-based systems may need fine tuning after installation to realize the potential savings in the range of 40%–70% rather than the realized savings of 10% typical of large pilot projects.

A study by Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory performed a [literature review](https://doi.org/10.2172/1129575) and estimated the savings achievable from advanced irrigation controllers. In the United States, average outdoor water use is 58,000 gallons per year per household, and of the 13.5 million single-family homes with automatic irrigation systems, a vast majority use an inefficient timer-based system. Based on 47 different references, advanced irrigation controllers could achieve water savings of 38% for SMSs and 15% for WBICs.

enriched_points.expanded_content: New sensor technologies improve precision in irrigation by providing real-time data on soil moisture, temperature, and weather conditions, enabling better water management and crop health.
AI and machine learning enhance irrigation efficiency by analyzing data patterns, predicting water needs, and automating irrigation schedules for optimal crop growth and resource conservation.

Renewable energy integration, such as solar-powered systems, reduces operational costs and environmental impact, promoting sustainable irrigation practices that align with climate-friendly agricultural solutions.
